explanation_md: The cast converts `a` to `double` before the division. Arithmetic conversions then promote `b` to `double` as well, so floating-point division occurs, giving 2.25. Without the cast, integer division would give 2.
`static_cast<int>` from a floating-point value always truncates toward zero — it does not round. So 7.9 becomes 7, not 8.

prompt_md: Which of the following is the primary advantage of `static_cast` over a C-style cast like `(int)x`?

options.b: static_cast is easier to search for in code and is rejected at compile time when the conversion is not meaningful
options.c: static_cast performs rounding instead of truncation
options.d: static_cast automatically promotes the result to double

explanation_md: `static_cast` uses a unique keyword syntax that is easy to grep, and it refuses to compile when the conversion cannot be verified as safe at compile time. C-style casts look like function calls and can silently do unsafe things. Neither cast performs rounding.

The division `x / y` is evaluated first in integer arithmetic, giving `1`. The cast then converts the integer `1` to the double `1.0`. To get 1.666..., the cast must wrap one of the operands before the division: `static_cast<double>(x) / y`.

prompt_md: When should you prefer `static_cast<int>(d)` over using list initialization `int i { d }` where `d` is a `double`?

options.a: When you want the compiler to report a narrowing error to catch accidental truncation
options.b: When you deliberately want to truncate the value and want to communicate that intent clearly
options.c: When you need the fractional part preserved in a separate variable
options.d: When you want automatic rounding to the nearest integer

explanation_md: List initialization `int i { d }` is intentionally stricter — it rejects narrowing conversions so that accidental truncation is caught. `static_cast` is for when you know you are truncating and want to silence the warning while signaling intent to the reader. It does not round.
